question: How many observers and how many scenes were used in the main eye-tracking search experiment, and what was the search target?
expected_evidence: Abstract L46; Introduction L77; Participants L83 ('Fourteen observers'); Stimuli L93 ('912 colour pictures').
ground_truth: 14 observers searched 912 outdoor urban scenes (half target-present, half target-absent) for a person/pedestrian.
why_strategic: Basic fact stated in both abstract and methods; tests whether the retriever lands on a definitional answer rather than a stray mention.
notes: Abstract is fragmented into sentence-titled nodes in the vanilla PDF arm, so 'low' not 'none'.

question: In the Dalal-Triggs target-features detector, what scanning-window size was used and how many pedestrians was the reported implementation trained on?
expected_evidence: Guidance by target features, L159 ('scanning window of 32×64 pixels and was trained on 2000 upright, unoccluded pedestrians').
ground_truth: A 32x64-pixel scanning window; trained on 2000 upright, unoccluded pedestrians (plus their left-right reflections).
why_strategic: The '32x64' spec is corrupted to '3264' in the raw PDF extraction (missing multiplication sign); directly probes glyph fidelity.
notes: REP-SENSITIVE: vanilla arm shows '3264'; Markdown arms show '32x64'.

question: According to Table 1, what is the scene-context model's Area-Under-Curve (AUC) in target-absent scenes, and how does it compare with the saliency model in the same condition?
expected_evidence: Table 1, target-absent block, L217 (Scene context .845) and L215 (Saliency .773).
ground_truth: Scene context AUC = .845 vs saliency AUC = .773 in target-absent scenes; scene context is higher by .072.
why_strategic: Reads specific cells from a table that exists as a clean Markdown table in our arms but as spilled, unstructured text (no table node) in the vanilla PDF arm.
notes: REP-SENSITIVE: vanilla has no Table 1 node; values spill as one-per-line raw text.

question: What does Figure 8 compare, and how are the two panels defined?
expected_evidence: Figure 8 caption, L205 ('Comparison between (a) the computationally defined scene context map and (b) the empirically defined context oracle map for a single image').
ground_truth: Figure 8 compares, for a single image, (a) the computationally-defined scene-context map vs (b) the empirically-defined context-oracle map (both thresholded at 20% of image area, with human-fixation dots).
why_strategic: Figure captions are addressable placeholder nodes in our arms but embedded in body text with no figure node in the vanilla arm; tests figure-content retrieval.
notes: REP-SENSITIVE: vanilla arm has zero figure nodes.

question: Does the paper report human behavioural performance (accuracy, reaction time) for the search task, or only computational model AUCs?
expected_evidence: Accuracy and eye movement statistics, L109 ('hits was 87%. The false alarm rate (fa)... was 3%... mean reaction time was 1050 ms... 1517 ms').
ground_truth: It reports both: human behavioural stats appear in 'Accuracy and eye movement statistics' - hits 87%, false-alarm rate 3%, mean RT 1050 ms (target-present) / 1517 ms (target-absent), ~3.5 vs 5.1 fixations. So the answer is 'yes, behavioural measures are reported', not only AUCs.
why_strategic: Tests whether the retriever finds the behavioural-results node rather than defaulting to the model-AUC results; the correct answer refutes a false premise.

question: The model combines three sources of guidance. Where is each source defined, and which section reports their combined performance?
expected_evidence: Guidance by saliency L141; Guidance by target features L153; Guidance by scene context features L177; combined L195; Modelling Results / Table 1 L207+.
ground_truth: Saliency (§Guidance by saliency, L141), target features (§Guidance by target features, L153), scene context (§Guidance by scene context features, L177); combined in §Guidance by a combined model of attention (L195), with combined performance in §Modelling Results / Table 1 (L207+).
why_strategic: Multi-section synthesis that mirrors the tree's structure; rewards hierarchy navigation over keyword matching.

question: What role does the 'context oracle' play across the paper, and does the purely computational combined model ever match it?
expected_evidence: Upper-bound rationale L189; construction L193; comparison L286 ('not significant in target-absent, t(405)=−1.233, p=.218'); oracle-substituted 96% L209.
ground_truth: DRAFT: The context oracle is an empirical UPPER BOUND on context-only guidance (L189), built from 7 participants' markings (L193). The computational combined model matches it in target-absent scenes - the difference is not significant (t(405)=−1.233, p=.218, L286) - though substituting the oracle for the computational context model still lifts performance to ~96% of human agreement (L209).
why_strategic: Synthesis across four sections plus a significance test; rewards connecting the oracle's purpose to the key comparison result.
notes: VERIFY the 'matches' reading rests on the non-significant target-absent difference.

question: What does the paper identify as the core limitation preventing any model from reaching human-level fixation prediction, and what future direction does it propose?
expected_evidence: Abstract L46; Discussion L294 ('map selectivity... region size of roughly 40%... tightly clustered'); Concluding Remarks L320.
ground_truth: The core limitation is selectivity/precision: human fixations cluster tightly in small regions while models select much broader regions - all models' accuracy falls off sharply as the selected region shrinks (a ~40% region is needed for the combined model to match human detection rate; L294). Proposed direction: future models that capture the mechanisms underlying the tight clustering of human search fixations (L320).
why_strategic: Reflective-discovery requiring synthesis of the limitation (Discussion) with the forward-looking claim (Concluding Remarks); resists a single-sentence lift.
